planting.note: Surface-sow seeds in fall directly onto dry, sandy, acidic soil in partial shade — this species thrives in the poor, lean conditions of pine barrens and open woodland edges that most plants cannot tolerate. Press seeds lightly into the soil without covering, as light aids germination, and allow winter cold to stratify them naturally. Avoid amending the soil with compost or fertilizer, as nutrient-rich conditions favor weeds and suppress this specialized native.

directions: Scatter seeds onto undisturbed, sandy, acidic soil beneath pines or oaks in fall, pressing them lightly against the surface without burying them. Cold stratification over winter will break dormancy and trigger germination once soil warms above 55°F in spring. Once established, this slow-growing aster requires virtually no supplemental water or care, making it ideal for dry shade gardens where other asters fail. Expect the characteristic blue-violet blooms to appear in late summer and fall, beginning in the second or third year from seed.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in fall into dry, sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun, matching the open pine flatwood conditions this southeastern native prefers. Rake seeds shallowly into the soil surface and allow winter rains to settle them in place. Avoid clay-heavy or moisture-retentive soils, which cause root rot; established plants are highly drought-tolerant and require little to no supplemental irrigation.

directions: Sow seeds in fall directly onto a prepared, lean, sandy or gravelly seedbed in full sun — this species from the dry southeastern coastal plain requires excellent drainage and will not persist in heavy soils. Lightly rake seeds into the top quarter-inch of soil and water once to settle; subsequent rains will carry the seeds through winter stratification. The distinctive silvery-woolly basal rosettes appear in early spring and the bright yellow flowers open in mid-spring, often before most summer wildflowers have emerged. Once established after the first season, plants are reliably drought-tolerant and will self-sow modestly into bare soil nearby.

planting.note: Scatter seeds in fall onto dry, open ground in partial sun to light shade along woodland edges or roadsides — this adaptable southeastern native tolerates a range of well-drained soils from sandy loam to clay as long as drainage is adequate. Press seeds into the soil surface and allow natural winter precipitation to provide cold stratification. Plants form arching, billowy mounds that are especially effective massed on dry slopes or as an informal hedge along woodland margins.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in fall by broadcasting them onto bare, dry, well-drained soil and pressing them lightly against the surface without covering. Cold winter temperatures stratify the seeds naturally, and germination occurs in spring when soil temperatures climb above 55°F. This vigorous and drought-tolerant aster will form full, arching clumps of small-leaved stems by the second season, then erupt in masses of tiny white flowers with yellow centers in late fall — a critical late-season nectar source for migrating monarchs and native bees. Divide clumps every few years in early spring to maintain vigor and control spread.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–2 weeks before the last frost date, surface-sowing onto moist seed-starting mix at cool room temperature (50–65°F), as this subantarctic species prefers cool germination conditions. Transplant outside after frost danger has passed into consistently moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade, sheltered from drying winds. This bold architectural plant performs best in cool, moist climates with reliably damp soil throughout the growing season.

directions: Start seeds at cool temperatures (50–65°F) by pressing them onto the surface of damp seed-starting mix without covering, then keep trays consistently moist until germination occurs in 10–21 days. Grow on in a cool, bright location and transplant outdoors in spring once frost risk has passed, into deep, consistently moist, organically rich soil in partial shade. This imposing South American perennial produces enormous paddle-shaped leaves topped by tall stems bearing clusters of large white daisy flowers in summer, followed by ornamental gray-woolly seed heads. Mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and prevent drought stress, which causes premature leaf collapse.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall directly onto moist, cool, humus-rich soil in a shaded or partly shaded position, as this alpine European species requires a period of cold stratification to germinate reliably — fall outdoor sowing provides this naturally. Press seeds lightly into the surface and keep the seedbed consistently moist; seeds will germinate the following spring as temperatures moderate. Cold stratification is essential: germination rates drop significantly for seeds sown without a winter chill period.

directions: Direct-sow in fall onto moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade, allowing the winter cold to provide the stratification this alpine species needs before germinating in spring. Seeds are small, so scatter them thinly over a prepared seedbed and press gently to ensure good soil contact without burying them deeply. This compact masterwort produces deeply divided, palmate leaves and delicate pincushion flower heads in early to midsummer — smaller in scale than A. major, making it well-suited to rock garden pockets and intimate shade plantings. Keep the soil consistently moist but never waterlogged, and divide established clumps every three to four years in early spring to maintain flowering vigor.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds around the last frost date in full sun in any well-drained, moderately fertile soil — common mallow is a tough, adaptable plant that germinates quickly across a wide range of soil types and is largely indifferent to soil quality. Sow seeds about a quarter-inch deep and thin seedlings to 12–18 inches apart once they reach 2 inches tall. Harvest young leaves and immature fruits continuously throughout the season to promote new growth and extend the edible harvest.

directions: Sow seeds directly in spring around the last frost date, pressing them a quarter-inch into the soil in a sunny location with average, well-drained ground — this adaptable plant thrives in poor soils with minimal fertility and water. Thin seedlings to 12–18 inches apart and begin harvesting the mild, slightly mucilaginous young leaves once plants reach 6 inches tall, using them raw in salads or cooked as a spinach substitute. The round, segmented fruits known as 'cheeses' are also edible and have a mild, slightly starchy flavor when harvested young and green. Plants will self-sow readily and may persist as short-lived perennials in warmer zones, so deadhead spent flowers if you wish to limit volunteer seedlings.
